Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Process flow (typical order fulfillment): RO -> CA -> RI -> PI -> QC -> PO -> GSL -> D IC and RP are financial/invoicing steps. Usually, IC happens after packing/dispatch, and RP happens after IC.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: I need to make reasonable assumptions based on standard process modeling for this scenario, while ensuring the constraints are logically consistent and cover the required structure. Lets map out a plausible flow: 1. RO starts the case. Occurs exactly once. 2. CA must happen after RO. Occurs exactly once.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: equivalence: (RO, D) to indicate every received order is dispatched, and (IC, RP) for invoicing/payment. always_before: (CA, RO), (RI, CA), (PI, RI), (QC, PI), (PO, QC), (GSL, PO), (D, GSL), (IC, D), (RP, IC). always_after: (RO, CA), (CA, RI), (RI, PI), (PI, QC), (QC, PO), (PO, GSL), (GSL, D), (D, IC), (IC, RP).

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: always_before: (A, B) means if A occurs, B must have occurred previously. So B -> A in execution order. always_after: (A, B) means if A occurs, B must occur later. So A -> B in execution order. directly_follows: (A, B) means B immediately follows A.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Lets verify the constraint definitions again to ensure Im using them correctly. Lets verify the directionality carefully.

Name: Backtracking - IND
Text: Self-Correction/Refinement during thought: Wait, always_before and always_after are exact inverses. Thats fine but maybe I should make always_after include some transitive ones. never_together: debates, decides on empty set. activ_freq: considers {0,1} for some but sticks to {1}.

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: In a standard order fulfillment, each activity occurs exactly once per order. using typical business process logic for order fulfillment.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Final check of the prompt. All constraints match the definitions. Directions are correct. Keys match exactly. Values are correct types. All good.
